story:
They say a lamppost is a beacon to everyone when you’ve forgotten where you came from or where you’re going. Margaret Grimes, ten yesterday and very nearly grown, wonders how one finds the beacon to begin with—until Monty, her polite, trouble-prone pet mouse with a talent for sniffing out lampposts, announces it’s glowing at the end of the garden. After the proper ritual—tap the left foot three times, spin twice clockwise—she tugs on her grey checked dress with the white collar (the coat pegs are still too high), and out they go.

The black Victorian lamppost with curvy, feathery scrollwork is not amused when she calls its trim funny. “I am distinguished,” it sniffs. Margaret apologizes, because she is well brought up, and grips the circular handrail as Lyle the Lamppost whirs them into a blue-morning spiral. Monty tucks into her pocket, whiskers out, while Margaret thinks crossly about short socks and pink bows. Lyle reminds her that not all children have soft, clean socks or kind mums, and she blushes at a second apology before they even arrive.

They land with a salty splash by golden sand and a pale blue sea. “I don’t like rust,” Lyle groans, wobbling to rest on the dry sand. Shouts crack the air: pirates, boots pounding, “Grab the child!” A turquoise hand rises from the shallows, offering a shell. “Blow, and I’ll rescue you.” It belongs to Thalassa, who calls herself goddess of the sea. Margaret, who was taught not to stare, thanks her, blows the shell, and is whisked onto the smooth noses of Delbert and Daphne, two dolphins as sleek as silver commas.

Thalassa twines seaweed into rope and loops it snugly around Margaret and the lamppost. With a flick, she bells a bright bubble over Lyle so he won’t rust, and bubbles bloom around Margaret and Monty so they can breathe as they plunge beneath the waves toward the North Sea palace of King Onda, brother-king to Neptune. A black submarine knifes across the dolphins’ path; pirates grin through their glass. Thalassa’s call booms through the water. A giant octopus, Narceus, unfurls a great sucker-studded arm and wraps the vessel like a toy. “Mayday!” the pirates squeak into their speaking tubes. That is the end of their nasty plan to snatch children and steal magic shells.

Lights rise like a city of stars—the Hyperborean sea realm. Thalassa promises her five brothers and parents will greet them, and that Narceus can shrink to the size of a pup. As they sweep in, five laughing brothers and her parents wave from coral balconies. Margaret’s neck aches from looking up at finned folk. Monty reminds her she has magic. “Three spins, say it neat—tail for the sea, legs back on land—mind your words or you’ll be stuck.” She twirls; pink light and flowers whirl off; she swims free with a gleaming tail.

King Onda, all silver hair and sea-strong shoulders, touches his flame-red trident to the throne edge. A pale blue wave brushes Margaret. “You have the protection of my trident,” he declares. He gives her a communicator shell so help will be instant whenever she calls. He taps the trident once more; Margaret’s tail shimmers back into legs for the journey home. Margaret, suddenly hungry for Mum’s sausage stew with fluffy potatoes and thick gravy, asks to go home before she worries her mum. Thalassa offers to guide them, but the King’s trident sends Margaret, Monty, and Lyle gliding through sea and air, while guards chart the way in case she calls again.

Back in the garden, Lyle gleams in the fading light like a secret. Beacons leave a glimmer of themselves for grown-ups, so no one frets when they slip away. Margaret thanks him for being both beacon and friend. She’ll draw it all in her art book; Mum will say she’s got a vivid imagination, and that’s all right. The back door opens. “Dinner time, Margaret—and a piece of cheese for Monty. Wash your hands; you’ve been polishing that wise old lamppost all afternoon.” Margaret waves to Lyle. He dims into the meadow-tangled shadows as she runs in, certain now how to leave—and how to come home.
